advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an operator-attested fixture whose attestation is a real ticket comment (not description text), or align the prompt/ADR/CLAUDE.md wording to say the attestation may live anywhere in the ticket body the verifier reads.
evidence: eval.yaml:160-162 embeds the attestation under a literal `## Comments` block inside `ticket_context`.
eval_solver.py:196-198 creates the ticket with `description=desc` (desc = ticket_context); no comments are added.
operations.py:64-69 renders REAL ticket comments under a `#### Comments` heading as `- {body}` bullets — structurally different from the description text the fixture uses.
completion_verifier.md:78-79 and ADR 0043:34-35 say the admissible evidence is 'a comment ... you read via `show_ticket`'.
Ticket 7226 AC4 explicitly accepts embedding the attestation in the description ('the text the solver injects as the disposable ticket's description'), so this is a known limitation, not an accident.
advisory.finding: The prompt, ADR, and CLAUDE.md consistently describe the operator attestation as living in a ticket COMMENT read via `show_ticket`, but the eval fixtures place it under a `## Comments` heading inside the ticket DESCRIPTION (the solver creates the ticket with description=ticket_context and no real comments), so the fixtures never exercise the real comment-rendering path.
scenarios: A regression in comment rendering (operations.py:_format_ticket) or in how show_ticket surfaces comments would not be caught by these fixtures, since they only ever place attestation text in the description.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end assertion (via the existing `_verify`/FakeRunner seam) that a FAIL finding carrying `remediation` survives `verify_completion` and validates against `completion_verdict.schema.json`.
evidence: test_remediation_round_trips_through_serialized_verdict calls `verdict.model_dump()` directly (tests/unit/test_verdict_finding_remediation.py:48) — it never routes the finding through the op pipeline.
The real serialization path is `finalize_outcome` with `structured.model_dump(exclude_none=True)` then `validate_structured` (src/rebar/llm/findings.py:313-320); the new test bypasses both.

An end-to-end helper already exists and is the standard seam for this op: `_verify(repo, tid, structured)` runs `verify_completion(... runner=FakeRunner(structured=structured))` (tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py:27-30).

ABSENCE: no test asserts that a FAIL finding carrying `remediation` survives `verify_completion` and validates against completion_verdict.schema.json — the one place a plumbing regression on the new field would surface.
advisory.finding: The new `remediation` field is verified only against the pydantic model in isolation (`model_dump()`), not through the real completion-verification op pipeline (`verify_completion`/`finalize_outcome(mode='structured')`/`validate_structured` against `completion_verdict.schema.json`). A regression where the op pipeline drops or rejects a finding's `remediation` end-to-end would not be caught.
scenarios: A future change tightens `finalize_outcome`/`validate_structured` (e.g. narrows the finding shape or stops preserving extra keys) and silently strips per-finding `remediation`.
Schema `additionalProperties` on the finding def is later flipped to false, rejecting the field at the op boundary.
advisory.suggested_fix: In tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py, add a test that calls `_verify(rebar_repo, _seed(rebar_repo), {"verdict": "FAIL", "findings": [{"criterion": "[operator-attested] deploy confirmed", "detail": "no attestation", "severity": "high", "dimension": "completion", "remediation": "Record proof: change id, outcome, when."}]})` and assert `r["findings"][0]["remediation"]` is preserved after the full normalize→validate op path.

coaching.coaching: Add a regression test covering remediation through the real verify_completion pipeline.
coaching.subject: remediation through the real verify_completion pipeline

coverage.approach_viability_note: 0 surviving high-priority finding(s), Pass-2 drop-rate 60% — the approach (not just nits) may be worth a second look (advisory; the verdict is unchanged).
